retrieval_only_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
retrieval_only_top.snippet:
Side-Channel Attacks: Dragonblood (2019)
The Attack
Vanhoef & Ronen (2019) discovered that the Hunting & Pecking 
loop in the original SAE spec had a variable number of iterations 
depending on the password.
By measuring execution time or cache access patterns, an attacker 
could determine how many iterations occurred, leaking 
information about the password.
This enabled offline dictionary attacks —partially defeating SAE's 
core security guarantee.
Affected: CVE -2019 -9494 (timing) and CVE -2019 -9496 (cache -
based).
The Fix
IEEE and the Wi -Fi Alliance patched the spec:
1. Replaced the loop with a constant -time hash -to-curve 
algorithm (using Legendre symbol computations that always 
run a fixed number of iterations).
2. Required constant -time ECC operations to prevent cache -
timing leakage.
3. SAE -PK (SAE with public key) was introduced as an 
additional defense for enterprise deployments.
Lesson: Cryptographic protocol security alone is insufficient —
side-channel resistance in implementation is equally critical.

retrieval_only_top.snippet:
What is SAE?
Definition
SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) is WPA3’s 
authentication protocol, replacing WPA2’s Pre -Shared Key 
(PSK) exchange.
It is based on the Dragonfly Key Exchange —a balanced 
Password Authenticated Key Exchange (PAKE) protocol 
standardized in RFC 7664.
Both peers prove knowledge of the password 
simultaneously, without transmitting the password or a value 
that allows offline derivation of it.
Key Cryptographic Properties
PAKE Protocol
Password is never sent —only proof of knowledge is 
exchanged.
Mutual Auth
Both client and AP authenticate each other 
simultaneously.
Forward Secrecy
Session keys are ephemeral; past sessions stay secure.
Offline Attack Resistance
Each auth attempt requires active interaction with the 
AP.

retrieval_only_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
retrieval_only_top.snippet:
Forward Secrecy in SAE
WPA2 —No Forward Secrecy
The PSK directly seeds the 4 -way handshake derivation:
PTK = PRF(PMK, nonces, MACs)
PMK = PSK
If the password is ever compromised —even years later —an 
attacker with recorded traffic can decrypt every past session 
retroactively.
WPA3 SAE —Perfect Forward 
Secrecy
SAE uses fresh ephemeral random scalars (r, m) for every 
authentication.
The session key K = r_A · r_B · PWE is derived from these 
ephemeral values.
Even if the password is later compromised, past session keys 
cannot be reconstructed —the ephemeral values were never 
stored and are computationally infeasible to recover.

rerank_top.snippet:
The Dragonfly Handshake: Overview
Client Access Point
1Hunting & Pecking
Both derive PWE (Password Element) from password using 
an elliptic curve point derivation algorithm.
2Commit Phase →
Both send a commit message: a scalar + elliptic curve 
element derived from ephemeral random values + PWE.
3Confirm Phase 
Both compute a shared PMK (Pairwise Master Key) and 
exchange confirm messages using HMAC to prove 
possession.
Shared PMK established —session keys derived via 4 -way handshake

retrieval_only_top.snippet:
WPA2 Weaknesses: Why SAE Was Needed
4-Way Handshake
WPA2 uses a 4 -way handshake to 
derive PTK. The handshake can be 
captured passively —attacker needs 
no interaction with the AP .
Offline Dictionary 
Attack
Once the handshake is captured, an 
attacker runs offline brute -force or 
dictionary attacks against the PSK 
without any network interaction.
No Forward Secrecy
The PSK is the root of trust. If the 
password is ever compromised, all 
previously captured sessions can be 
decrypted retroactively.

rerank_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
rerank_top.snippet:
Commit & Confirm Phases: The Math
Commit Phase
Each peer picks random scalars r, m ∈[1, q -1].
Compute scalar:
scalar = (r + m) mod q
Compute element:
element = -(m · PWE)
Send (scalar, element) to the other peer.
These values are computationally bound to the password (via 
PWE) and the random values, but reveal nothing about either.
Confirm Phase
Compute the shared secret K:
K = r · (peer_scalar · PWE + peer_element)
Derive PMK and KCK from K using a KDF.
Compute confirm token:
confirm = HMAC -SHA256(KCK,
scalar_i || scalar_r || element_i
|| element_r || MAC_i || MAC_r)
Exchange confirm tokens and verify. If both match, 
authentication succeeds.

rerank_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
rerank_top.snippet:
Forward Secrecy in SAE
WPA2 —No Forward Secrecy
The PSK directly seeds the 4 -way handshake derivation:
PTK = PRF(PMK, nonces, MACs)
PMK = PSK
If the password is ever compromised —even years later —an 
attacker with recorded traffic can decrypt every past session 
retroactively.
WPA3 SAE —Perfect Forward 
Secrecy
SAE uses fresh ephemeral random scalars (r, m) for every 
authentication.
The session key K = r_A · r_B · PWE is derived from these 
ephemeral values.
Even if the password is later compromised, past session keys 
cannot be reconstructed —the ephemeral values were never 
stored and are computationally infeasible to recover.

rerank_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
rerank_top.snippet:
What is SAE?
Definition
SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) is WPA3’s 
authentication protocol, replacing WPA2’s Pre -Shared Key 
(PSK) exchange.
It is based on the Dragonfly Key Exchange —a balanced 
Password Authenticated Key Exchange (PAKE) protocol 
standardized in RFC 7664.
Both peers prove knowledge of the password 
simultaneously, without transmitting the password or a value 
that allows offline derivation of it.
Key Cryptographic Properties
PAKE Protocol
Password is never sent —only proof of knowledge is 
exchanged.
Mutual Auth
Both client and AP authenticate each other 
simultaneously.
Forward Secrecy
Session keys are ephemeral; past sessions stay secure.
Offline Attack Resistance
Each auth attempt requires active interaction with the 
AP.

retrieval_only_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
retrieval_only_top.snippet:
Commit & Confirm Phases: The Math
Commit Phase
Each peer picks random scalars r, m ∈[1, q -1].
Compute scalar:
scalar = (r + m) mod q
Compute element:
element = -(m · PWE)
Send (scalar, element) to the other peer.
These values are computationally bound to the password (via 
PWE) and the random values, but reveal nothing about either.
Confirm Phase
Compute the shared secret K:
K = r · (peer_scalar · PWE + peer_element)
Derive PMK and KCK from K using a KDF.
Compute confirm token:
confirm = HMAC -SHA256(KCK,
scalar_i || scalar_r || element_i
|| element_r || MAC_i || MAC_r)
Exchange confirm tokens and verify. If both match, 
authentication succeeds.

retrieval_only_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
retrieval_only_top.snippet:
Why Both Peers Compute the Same K
Algebraic Proof of Shared Secret
Alice computes:
K_A = r_A · (scalar_B · PWE + element_B)
= r_A · ((r_B + m_B) · PWE + ( -(m_B · PWE)))
= r_A · (r_B · PWE + m_B · PWE -m_B · PWE)
= r_A · r_B · PWEBob computes:
K_B = r_B · (scalar_A · PWE + element_A)
= r_B · ((r_A + m_A) · PWE + ( -(m_A · PWE)))
= r_B · (r_A · PWE + m_A · PWE -m_A · PWE)
= r_B · r_A · PWE
K_A = K_B = r_A · r_B · PWE    →    Both arrive at the same shared secret!
The mask value m cancels out algebraically. An eavesdropper sees (scalar, element) but cannot recover r (protected by ECDLP), soK 
remains secret.

rerank_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
rerank_top.snippet:
Side-Channel Attacks: Dragonblood (2019)
The Attack
Vanhoef & Ronen (2019) discovered that the Hunting & Pecking 
loop in the original SAE spec had a variable number of iterations 
depending on the password.
By measuring execution time or cache access patterns, an attacker 
could determine how many iterations occurred, leaking 
information about the password.
This enabled offline dictionary attacks —partially defeating SAE's 
core security guarantee.
Affected: CVE -2019 -9494 (timing) and CVE -2019 -9496 (cache -
based).
The Fix
IEEE and the Wi -Fi Alliance patched the spec:
1. Replaced the loop with a constant -time hash -to-curve 
algorithm (using Legendre symbol computations that always 
run a fixed number of iterations).
2. Required constant -time ECC operations to prevent cache -
timing leakage.
3. SAE -PK (SAE with public key) was introduced as an 
additional defense for enterprise deployments.
Lesson: Cryptographic protocol security alone is insufficient —
side-channel resistance in implementation is equally critical.

rerank_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
rerank_top.snippet:
Side-Channel Attacks: Dragonblood (2019)
The Attack
Vanhoef & Ronen (2019) discovered that the Hunting & Pecking 
loop in the original SAE spec had a variable number of iterations 
depending on the password.
By measuring execution time or cache access patterns, an attacker 
could determine how many iterations occurred, leaking 
information about the password.
This enabled offline dictionary attacks —partially defeating SAE's 
core security guarantee.
Affected: CVE -2019 -9494 (timing) and CVE -2019 -9496 (cache -
based).
The Fix
IEEE and the Wi -Fi Alliance patched the spec:
1. Replaced the loop with a constant -time hash -to-curve 
algorithm (using Legendre symbol computations that always 
run a fixed number of iterations).
2. Required constant -time ECC operations to prevent cache -
timing leakage.
3. SAE -PK (SAE with public key) was introduced as an 
additional defense for enterprise deployments.
Lesson: Cryptographic protocol security alone is insufficient —
side-channel resistance in implementation is equally critical.

The Dragonfly Handshake: Overview
Client Access Point
1Hunting & Pecking
Both derive PWE (Password Element) from password using 
an elliptic curve point derivation algorithm.
2Commit Phase →
Both send a commit message: a scalar + elliptic curve 
element derived from ephemeral random values + PWE.
3Confirm Phase 
Both compute a shared PMK (Pairwise Master Key) and 
exchange confirm messages using HMAC to prove 
possession.
Shared PMK established —session keys derived via 4 -way handshake

retrieval_only_top.snippet:
Forward Secrecy in SAE
WPA2 —No Forward Secrecy
The PSK directly seeds the 4 -way handshake derivation:
PTK = PRF(PMK, nonces, MACs)
PMK = PSK
If the password is ever compromised —even years later —an 
attacker with recorded traffic can decrypt every past session 
retroactively.
WPA3 SAE —Perfect Forward 
Secrecy
SAE uses fresh ephemeral random scalars (r, m) for every 
authentication.
The session key K = r_A · r_B · PWE is derived from these 
ephemeral values.
Even if the password is later compromised, past session keys 
cannot be reconstructed —the ephemeral values were never 
stored and are computationally infeasible to recover.

rerank_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
rerank_top.snippet:
Side-Channel Attacks: Dragonblood (2019)
The Attack
Vanhoef & Ronen (2019) discovered that the Hunting & Pecking 
loop in the original SAE spec had a variable number of iterations 
depending on the password.
By measuring execution time or cache access patterns, an attacker 
could determine how many iterations occurred, leaking 
information about the password.
This enabled offline dictionary attacks —partially defeating SAE's 
core security guarantee.
Affected: CVE -2019 -9494 (timing) and CVE -2019 -9496 (cache -
based).
The Fix
IEEE and the Wi -Fi Alliance patched the spec:
1. Replaced the loop with a constant -time hash -to-curve 
algorithm (using Legendre symbol computations that always 
run a fixed number of iterations).
2. Required constant -time ECC operations to prevent cache -
timing leakage.
3. SAE -PK (SAE with public key) was introduced as an 
additional defense for enterprise deployments.
Lesson: Cryptographic protocol security alone is insufficient —
side-channel resistance in implementation is equally critical.

The Dragonfly Handshake: Overview
Client Access Point
1Hunting & Pecking
Both derive PWE (Password Element) from password using 
an elliptic curve point derivation algorithm.
2Commit Phase →
Both send a commit message: a scalar + elliptic curve 
element derived from ephemeral random values + PWE.
3Confirm Phase 
Both compute a shared PMK (Pairwise Master Key) and 
exchange confirm messages using HMAC to prove 
possession.
Shared PMK established —session keys derived via 4 -way handshake

Side-Channel Attacks: Dragonblood (2019)
The Attack
Vanhoef & Ronen (2019) discovered that the Hunting & Pecking 
loop in the original SAE spec had a variable number of iterations 
depending on the password.
By measuring execution time or cache access patterns, an attacker 
could determine how many iterations occurred, leaking 
information about the password.
This enabled offline dictionary attacks —partially defeating SAE's 
core security guarantee.
Affected: CVE -2019 -9494 (timing) and CVE -2019 -9496 (cache -
based).
The Fix
IEEE and the Wi -Fi Alliance patched the spec:
1. Replaced the loop with a constant -time hash -to-curve 
algorithm (using Legendre symbol computations that always 
run a fixed number of iterations).
2. Required constant -time ECC operations to prevent cache -
timing leakage.
3. SAE -PK (SAE with public key) was introduced as an 
additional defense for enterprise deployments.
Lesson: Cryptographic protocol security alone is insufficient —
side-channel resistance in implementation is equally critical.

retrieval_only_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
retrieval_only_top.snippet:
Forward Secrecy in SAE
WPA2 —No Forward Secrecy
The PSK directly seeds the 4 -way handshake derivation:
PTK = PRF(PMK, nonces, MACs)
PMK = PSK
If the password is ever compromised —even years later —an 
attacker with recorded traffic can decrypt every past session 
retroactively.
WPA3 SAE —Perfect Forward 
Secrecy
SAE uses fresh ephemeral random scalars (r, m) for every 
authentication.
The session key K = r_A · r_B · PWE is derived from these 
ephemeral values.
Even if the password is later compromised, past session keys 
cannot be reconstructed —the ephemeral values were never 
stored and are computationally infeasible to recover.

The Dragonfly Handshake: Overview
Client Access Point
1Hunting & Pecking
Both derive PWE (Password Element) from password using 
an elliptic curve point derivation algorithm.
2Commit Phase →
Both send a commit message: a scalar + elliptic curve 
element derived from ephemeral random values + PWE.
3Confirm Phase 
Both compute a shared PMK (Pairwise Master Key) and 
exchange confirm messages using HMAC to prove 
possession.
Shared PMK established —session keys derived via 4 -way handshake

rerank_top.title: WPA3_SAE_Presentation.pdf
rerank_top.snippet:
Hunting & Pecking: Deriving the Password 
Element (PWE)
Algorithm (using NIST P -256 / Group 19)
• Input: password, MAC addresses of client and AP.
• Compute seed = H(max(MAC_A, MAC_B) || min(MAC_A, MAC_B) || 
password || counter) where H = SHA -256.
• Derive a candidate x -coordinate on the elliptic curve from the seed.
• Check if x is a valid point on the curve (i.e., x³ + ax + b is a quadratic 
residue mod p).
• If valid, set PWE = point on curve. If not, increment counter and 
repeat.
• PWE is deterministic and computationally indistinguishable from 
random.
Why This Matters
The PWE is a shared secret derived from the 
password. It is a point on an elliptic curve —an 
adversary who does not know the password cannot 
compute PWE, making offline attacks impossible.
Timing Side -Channel Risk
The number of loop iterations depends on the 
password and MAC addresses. Variable iteration 
counts can leak timing information —this was the 
basis of Dragonblood attacks (CVE -2019 -9494).
